text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr James Patel
Riverside Medical Practice

12 Millbank, London SW1P 3GE
Dear Dr James Patel
Alex Morgan, DOB: 14-03-1990, Hospital No: H927461 NHS No. 7485209163
Flat 3, 21 Kingfisher Close, London, SE10 8AB 

Diagnoses/summary: Combined generalised and focal epilepsy (patient has both generalised and focal epilepsy), longstanding; learning points from prior work-up include mixed semiologies consistent with dual network involvement

MRI brain (2019) reported normal; no structural focus identified

EEG history: background normal; frequent interictal epileptiform activity recorded without captured clinical events; most recent EEG (Aug 2025) noted sporadic generalised spike–wave and independent left temporal sharp waves with no recorded clinical counterpart

Previous medication: sodium valproate (weight gain, tremor), carbamazepine (rash), lamotrigine (partial benefit), levetiracetam (irritability), topiramate (cognitive slowing), zonisamide (reduced appetite), clobazam (sedation), perampanel (mood change)

Not taken: lacosamide, brivaracetam, cannabidiol (Epidyolex), cenobamate, rufinamide, tiagabine, pregabalin

Comorbidities: mild asthma; vitamin D insufficiency; no known cardiac history

I reviewed Alex in clinic today, accompanied by his partner. He reports two distinct seizure patterns:
- Generalised: brief morning myoclonic jerks several times per week, and three generalised tonic–clonic seizures in the past six months, typically on sleep deprivation.
- Focal: episodes of rising epigastric sensation followed by behavioural arrest and lip smacking lasting 60–90 seconds, occurring roughly once every 2–3 weeks, with post-event confusion for 10–15 minutes.

He keeps a diary and notes that alcohol and irregular sleep are common precipitants. He is currently driving-ineligible and understands DVLA guidance. He denies recent injuries. Mood is stable; no active safety concerns. He is interested in future family planning and asks about medication options compatible with this.

Current medication:
- Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily
- Levetiracetam 1 g twice daily
- Vitamin D3 1000 IU daily
- Salbutamol inhaler PRN

Examination today was unremarkable; no focal neurological deficits. Weight stable. Bloods from July 2025: FBC, U&E, LFTs within reference limits; vitamin D marginally low (repletion initiated). ECG normal.

EEG context and frequency statement: His most recent EEG demonstrated interictal epileptiform abnormalities without any recorded seizure during the study, aligning with his report of intermittent events outside the recording window and supporting the clinical impression of combined generalised and focal epilepsy.

Plan:
- Optimise antiseizure therapy: discuss transition from levetiracetam to brivaracetam to address irritability risk; commence cross-taper if agreed after patient information provided and consent obtained. If suboptimal control persists, consider add-on lacosamide targeting focal-onset events. We will avoid sodium valproate given prior adverse effects and his wish to minimise metabolic side effects.
- Lifestyle: reinforce sleep regularity, alcohol minimisation, and hydration; continue seizure diary with differentiation of generalised vs focal events.
- Safety: DVLA regulations reinforced; SUDEP and general safety measures discussed (supervised bathing, avoid heights/open water when alone).
- Investigations: arrange repeat MRI epilepsy protocol with 3T if feasible; consider ambulatory EEG if diary suggests clustering to better phenotype events temporally.
- Follow-up: nurse-led telephone review at 4 weeks to assess tolerability and frequency; clinic review in 3 months or sooner if deterioration or adverse effects.

Thank you for your ongoing care and prescriptions support. Please do not hesitate to contact me if queries arise or if earlier review is needed.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: brief morning myoclonic jerks several times per week

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis:
The letter details current seizure patterns:
- Generalised: 'brief morning myoclonic jerks several times per week' and 'three generalised tonic–clonic seizures in the past six months'.
- Focal: events 'occurring roughly once every 2–3 weeks'.
Applying the rule to choose the highest frequency, the myoclonic jerks ('several times per week') are more frequent than focal seizures (about 1 per 2–3 weeks) and GTCs (3 in 6 months). Because 'several' is vague, use 'multiple' as allowed. Therefore, the final label is 'multiple per week'.
seizure_frequency_number: brief morning myoclonic jerks several times per week
reference: Interictal discharges on EEG; no ictal correlate

flaws.description: Gold (multiple per week) disagrees with Reference (unknown)
